cards.back: Any innovation that includes a program as an integral part of its function. Can be physical, nonphysical software, or a nonphysical concept.
A computing innovation that has a tangible physical form and includes a program as integral to its function. Examples: self-driving car, smart thermostat, fitness tracker.
A computing innovation that exists as a standalone software application with no physical form. Examples: picture editing software, photo filter app, word processor.
A computing innovation that exists as a system or model enabled by computing — larger than a single application. Examples: e-commerce, ride-sharing service, social media platform.
A preference or prejudice embedded in a computing innovation that disadvantages certain groups of users. Often occurs unintentionally when the development team lacks diverse perspectives.

The practice of communicating with potential users during development to gather information and feedback. Helps the team understand the program's purpose from diverse perspectives and develop an innovation that fully incorporates those perspectives.

A recognized collaboration model where one programmer writes code while another observes and provides real-time feedback. Both roles actively contribute — the reviewer catches errors the writer might miss.

Digital tools that support collaboration by allowing programmers to share code and documents and provide feedback on ideas regardless of location or time zone. Examples: version control systems, shared coding platforms, document editors.

The process of working toward a decision or solution that all team members can support, even if it is not everyone's first choice. Ensures all perspectives are incorporated and the whole team is committed to the direction.

Collaboration that includes diverse perspectives helps avoid bias in the development of computing innovations — when the team reflects a range of backgrounds and experiences, assumptions that disadvantage certain users are more likely to be identified and corrected.
